enrichment.short_description: Open-source, self-hosted video and photo sharing platform for building your own YouTube-style website.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: ClipBucket is an open-source PHP script that lets anyone launch their own video and photo sharing website. The current release, ClipBucket V5, is maintained by Oxygenz, which has developed the codebase since 2016 after the original ClipBucket project stalled and was later archived.

The self-hosted Community edition is free to download from GitHub; Oxygenz also offers an optional managed SaaS hosting plan and paid setup/migration services for people who don't want to run their own server.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: ClipBucket suits organizations and communities that want a self-hosted, branded alternative to public video platforms — for internal video portals, niche video communities, or educational content libraries — and are comfortable running a PHP application on their own server.

features.title: Video & Photo Sharing Platform
features.description: Self-hosted PHP application for launching a full video and photo sharing website in minutes.

features.title: Video Conversion (MP4/HLS)
features.description: Automatically converts uploaded video into web-friendly MP4 and HLS formats.

features.title: 4K & Chromecast Support
features.description: Handles 4K video playback and Chromecast-compatible streaming.

features.title: Subtitles & Multilingual Audio
features.description: Supports video subtitles and multiple audio tracks for international audiences.

features.title: Social Features
features.description: Includes user groups, playlists, collections, friend requests, and private messaging.

features.title: Multi-Server Infrastructure
features.description: Can be scaled across multiple servers for larger deployments.

pros: Free, self-hosted Community edition with full source access on GitHub
Actively maintained (current version 5.5.0) after taking over from the original abandoned project
Rich social and video features out of the box (playlists, groups, messaging)
Supports modern formats: 4K, HLS conversion, Chromecast
cons: Requires you to provide and manage your own PHP hosting
The original ClipBucket project (pre-2016) was abandoned and archived in December 2022, which can cause confusion with the current Oxygenz-maintained V5
Managed hosting, priority support, and custom development require the paid SaaS plan

faqs.question: Is ClipBucket free?
faqs.answer: The self-hosted Community edition is free via GitHub. Oxygenz also sells an optional managed SaaS hosting plan.

faqs.question: Is ClipBucket still maintained?
faqs.answer: Yes. Oxygenz has maintained ClipBucket V5 since 2016, separate from the original ClipBucket project, which was archived in December 2022.

faqs.question: What does ClipBucket require to run?
faqs.answer: It's a PHP-based script that needs your own server or hosting environment.

faqs.question: Does ClipBucket support 4K video?
faqs.answer: Yes, along with HLS conversion and Chromecast compatibility.

use_cases.title: Private/branded video community
use_cases.description: Run a self-hosted video-sharing site under your own brand instead of relying on third-party platforms.

use_cases.title: Internal or educational video portal
use_cases.description: Host training, lecture, or internal communication videos on infrastructure you control.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: ClipBucket is an open-source, self-hosted PHP platform for building your own video and photo sharing website, free for self-hosting.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source UPnP/DLNA media server that streams your personal media library to TVs, consoles, and mobile devices.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Gerbera is a free UPnP/DLNA media server that streams music, video, and photos to devices on a home network, including smart TVs, game consoles, and phones. It is a community-driven continuation of the MediaTomb project, built on the MediaTomb 0.12.1 codebase since 2016 and released under the GPLv2 license.

The server includes automatic metadata extraction, optional on-the-fly transcoding, and a JavaScript-based import layer for customizing how media is organized, all managed through a web-based configuration interface.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Gerbera is aimed at home users and self-hosters running Linux who want to serve a local media collection to standards-based DLNA/UPnP clients without relying on a proprietary media server.

features.title: UPnP/DLNA Streaming
features.description: Streams media to compatible smart TVs, game consoles, and mobile devices over the local network.

features.title: Automatic Metadata Extraction
features.description: Pulls metadata from music and video files automatically.

features.title: On-the-Fly Transcoding
features.description: Optionally converts media formats to match what the client device supports.

features.title: JavaScript Import Scripting
features.description: Customize how your library is organized using JavaScript-based import rules.

features.title: Web-Based Configuration UI
features.description: Manage the server through a browser-based interface.

features.title: Continuation of MediaTomb
features.description: Actively maintained fork that carries forward the well-known MediaTomb project.

pros: Completely free and open source (GPLv2)
Actively maintained, unlike the original MediaTomb project it forked from
Broad device compatibility through standard UPnP/DLNA
Scriptable metadata and import behavior for advanced customization
cons: UPnP/DLNA network setup can be technical for non-technical users
Advanced customization requires editing JavaScript import scripts
Designed for local network use, with no built-in remote/cloud access

faqs.question: What is Gerbera?
faqs.answer: A UPnP/DLNA media server for streaming media to compatible devices on your home network.

faqs.question: Is Gerbera free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is free and open source under the GPLv2 license.

faqs.question: Is Gerbera related to MediaTomb?
faqs.answer: Yes, Gerbera continues development from the MediaTomb 0.12.1 codebase, starting in 2016.

faqs.question: Does Gerbera support transcoding?
faqs.answer: Yes, it can optionally transcode media on the fly for device compatibility.

use_cases.title: Home media streaming
use_cases.description: Stream a personal music, video, or photo library to smart TVs and game consoles.
use_cases.audience: Home users with local media libraries

use_cases.title: Self-hosted DLNA server
use_cases.description: Add DLNA/UPnP streaming to an existing Linux server or NAS.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Gerbera is a free, open-source UPnP/DLNA media server for streaming your music, video, and photo library to TVs and other devices.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source streaming media server for internet radio and audio/video broadcasting, maintained by Xiph.Org.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Icecast is free server software for streaming multimedia, first released in 1999 as an open alternative to proprietary streaming audio technologies of the time. It is maintained by the Xiph.Org Foundation, the same organization behind formats like Vorbis, Opus, FLAC, and Theora, and is distributed under the GNU GPL version 2.

Icecast 2 supports Ogg Vorbis, Ogg Theora, Opus, WebM, and MP3 streams, and can be used to run internet radio stations, jukeboxes, and other live audio or video streaming services. Recent versions add a web admin interface with dark mode, listener count tracking, TLS support, and X-Forwarded-For proxy handling.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Icecast is built for broadcasters, hobbyist DJs, community radio operators, and developers who need a lightweight, standards-based server for distributing live audio or video streams over HTTP.

features.title: Internet Radio & Streaming Server
features.description: Serves live audio and video streams in Ogg Vorbis, Opus, WebM, and MP3 formats.

features.title: Web-Based Admin Interface
features.description: Includes listener statistics, dark mode, and server management through a browser.

features.title: TLS/SSL Support
features.description: Supports encrypted connections for both source and listener clients.

features.title: X-Forwarded-For Support
features.description: Correctly logs listener information when running behind a reverse proxy.

features.title: Multi-Format Streaming
features.description: Handles both audio and video streams for radio, podcasts, and live broadcasts.

features.title: Long-Standing, Stable Codebase
features.description: In active use by radio stations and streaming projects since 1999.

pros: Free and open source under the GPLv2
Lightweight and stable, with a long production track record
Supports a wide range of audio and video formats
Compatible with a large ecosystem of source client software (e.g., BUTT, ffmpeg)
cons: No built-in transcoding — relies on external encoders/source clients
Admin interface is functional but basic compared to commercial platforms
Requires a separate source client to broadcast to the server

faqs.question: What is Icecast used for?
faqs.answer: Running internet radio stations and streaming live audio or video.

faqs.question: Is Icecast free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is free software licensed under the GPLv2.

faqs.question: Who maintains Icecast?
faqs.answer: The Xiph.Org Foundation.

faqs.question: Does Icecast support video streaming?
faqs.answer: Yes, formats such as WebM and Ogg Theora are supported alongside audio.

use_cases.title: Internet radio station
use_cases.description: Broadcast a live audio stream to listeners over HTTP.

use_cases.title: Live event or podcast streaming
use_cases.description: Stream live audio or video from an event or show to an online audience.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Icecast is a free, open-source streaming media server for internet radio and audio/video broadcasting, maintained by the Xiph.Org Foundation.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source self-hosted karaoke party system with a mobile-friendly song browser and queue.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Karaoke Eternal is a self-hosted, open-source karaoke system built for parties. It runs a server on your own hardware and lets guests find and queue songs directly from their phone's browser, with no app install required.

It supports MP3+G files and MP4 videos with music-synced visualizations, offers QR-code joining, and can run multiple simultaneous password-protected rooms with independent queues. The project states it includes no ads or telemetry.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Karaoke Eternal is aimed at people hosting karaoke parties at home or in small venues who already own karaoke media files and want a self-hosted, browser-based queue system instead of proprietary karaoke machines or apps.

features.title: Self-Hosted Karaoke Server
features.description: Runs entirely on your own hardware and is playable from any browser on the network.

features.title: Mobile Song Browser
features.description: Guests search and queue songs from their own phone's browser.

features.title: Multi-Room Support
features.description: Runs multiple simultaneous, password-protected rooms with independent queues.

features.title: QR Code Join
features.description: Guests join a room instantly by scanning a QR code, no app required.

features.title: MP3+G & MP4 Support
features.description: Plays MP3+G karaoke tracks and MP4 videos with synced visualizations.

features.title: No Ads or Telemetry
features.description: The project states it collects no telemetry and shows no ads.

pros: Completely free and open source
No app install needed — guests use their phone's browser
Supports multiple simultaneous password-protected rooms
No ads or telemetry
cons: You must supply your own karaoke media files — no built-in song library
Self-hosting requires basic server and network setup knowledge
No official native mobile apps; browser-only

faqs.question: Is Karaoke Eternal free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is fully free and open source.

faqs.question: Do guests need to install an app?
faqs.answer: No, it runs in any modern mobile browser.

faqs.question: What file formats does it support?
faqs.answer: MP3+G tracks and MP4 video files.

faqs.question: Can I run multiple karaoke rooms at once?
faqs.answer: Yes, with independent, password-protected queues.

use_cases.title: Home karaoke party
use_cases.description: Let guests queue their own songs from their phones during a home party.

use_cases.title: Small venue karaoke night
use_cases.description: Run a self-hosted queue system instead of a proprietary karaoke machine.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Karaoke Eternal is a free, open-source self-hosted karaoke system letting guests browse and queue songs from their phone's browser.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source media center software for playing and organizing video, music, and photos across many platforms.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Kodi is a free and open-source home theater/media center application originally created in 2003 as XBMC and renamed Kodi in 2014. It is developed by the non-profit XBMC Foundation and a global community of volunteer contributors, and is released under the GPLv2 license.

Kodi plays and organizes local and network video, music, and photo files, and can be extended with skins and add-ons for streaming services, live TV/DVR backends, weather, and remote control. It's available for Windows, macOS, Linux, Android, iOS, and tvOS.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Kodi suits home theater PC (HTPC) enthusiasts, cord-cutters, and anyone wanting a single, customizable hub for a personal video, music, and photo library across desktop, mobile, and TV devices.

features.title: Cross-Platform Media Center
features.description: Available for Windows, macOS, Linux, Android, iOS, and tvOS.

features.title: Local & Network Playback
features.description: Plays video, music, and photo files from local storage or network shares.

features.title: Live TV & DVR Integration
features.description: Connects to backends like Tvheadend, MythTV, and NextPVR for live TV and recording.

features.title: Skinning Engine
features.description: Fully customizable interface themes via user-created skins.

features.title: Add-on Ecosystem
features.description: Extend functionality with community add-ons for streaming, weather, and more.

features.title: Game Emulator Support
features.description: Play games through emulators with controller support.

pros: Completely free and open source (GPLv2)
Available on nearly every major platform
Large, mature add-on and skin ecosystem
Over two decades of active development and community support
cons: Initial setup and configuration can be complex for first-time users
Third-party add-on quality varies and some can carry legal streaming risks
Live TV/DVR functionality requires separate backend software

faqs.question: Is Kodi free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Kodi is completely free and open source.

faqs.question: Who develops Kodi?
faqs.answer: The XBMC Foundation, a non-profit run by volunteer developers.

faqs.question: What platforms does Kodi support?
faqs.answer: Windows, macOS, Linux, Android, iOS, and tvOS.

faqs.question: Does Kodi include streaming content itself?
faqs.answer: No, Kodi is a media player and organizer; content comes from your own files or third-party add-ons.

use_cases.title: Home theater PC (HTPC)
use_cases.description: Turn a PC or set-top device into a full media center for TV playback.
use_cases.audience: Home users with local media libraries

use_cases.title: Centralized media hub
use_cases.description: Consolidate video, music, and photo collections into one interface across devices.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Kodi is a free, open-source media center for playing and organizing video, music, and photos on Windows, macOS, Linux, Android, and iOS.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, self-hosted media server and proxy for routing RTSP, RTMP, HLS, WebRTC, and SRT streams.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: MediaMTX is a ready-to-use, open-source live media router that acts as both a server and a proxy. It handles publishing, reading, proxying, forwarding, recording, and playback of real-time video and audio streams across RTSP, RTMP, HLS, WebRTC, and SRT.
The project ships as a single self-contained binary with no external dependencies, is hosted on GitHub with a large community (20K+ stars), and is free to self-host with no licensing cost.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: MediaMTX is aimed at developers, security/surveillance integrators, and technical self-hosters who need to route live camera or streaming sources between different protocols without a graphical setup.

features.title: Multi-Protocol Streaming
features.description: Supports RTSP, RTMP, HLS, WebRTC, and SRT for publishing and reading streams.

features.title: Publish, Read, Proxy & Forward
features.description: Flexible live-stream routing between sources, clients, and other servers.

features.title: Stream Recording
features.description: Records incoming streams to disk in common formats.

features.title: REST API
features.description: Manage streams and server configuration programmatically.

features.title: Single-Binary Deployment
features.description: Ships as one self-contained binary with no external dependencies.

features.title: Active Open-Source Development
features.description: Frequent releases and a large GitHub community.

pros: Free and open source
Supports nearly every major live-streaming protocol
Simple single-binary deployment
Actively maintained with frequent releases
cons: No built-in graphical admin UI — configuration is file/API-based
Best suited to technical users familiar with streaming protocols
No official managed/hosted service

faqs.question: What is MediaMTX used for?
faqs.answer: Routing and serving live video/audio streams across protocols like RTSP, RTMP, HLS, WebRTC, and SRT.

faqs.question: Is MediaMTX free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is open source and free to self-host.

faqs.question: Does MediaMTX support recording?
faqs.answer: Yes, it can record incoming streams to disk.

faqs.question: Can MediaMTX be controlled programmatically?
faqs.answer: Yes, via its REST API.

use_cases.title: IP camera/NVR stream routing
use_cases.description: Convert and route camera streams between protocols for different clients.

use_cases.title: Custom live-streaming pipelines
use_cases.description: Build applications that publish, proxy, or record live streams.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: MediaMTX is a free, open-source media server and proxy for routing RTSP, RTMP, HLS, WebRTC, and SRT video streams.

enrichment.short_description: Streaming media server toolkit with a free open-source edition and commercially licensed editions for OTT delivery.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: MistServer is a full-featured streaming media toolkit for OTT (over-the-top internet streaming), originally developed by the Dutch company DDVTech B.V. (founded 2009). It supports a wide range of modern streaming protocols, including WebRTC, WHIP, SRT, RIST, and HLS.

MistServer offers a free, self-hostable open-source edition licensed under AGPLv3, alongside commercially licensed editions for organizations that need proprietary usage rights, additional features, or vendor support.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: MistServer targets developers, system integrators, and media companies building OTT streaming infrastructure who need broad protocol support and the option to scale into a commercially supported deployment.

features.title: Multi-Protocol Streaming
features.description: Supports WebRTC, WHIP, SRT, RIST, HLS, and other modern streaming protocols.

features.title: Open-Source Edition
features.description: Free, self-hostable edition licensed under AGPLv3.

features.title: Access Control
features.description: Restrict who can publish or view streams.

features.title: Analytics Integration
features.description: Connects with analytics tooling for stream monitoring.

features.title: Reverse Proxy Support
features.description: Supports flexible network topologies for deployment behind proxies.

features.title: Commercial Licensing
features.description: Paid licenses available for proprietary use and vendor support.

pros: Genuinely free, self-hostable open-source edition
Supports a very wide range of modern streaming protocols
Backed by an established streaming technology company with commercial support options
Actively developed with regular releases
cons: Full feature set and vendor support require a commercial license
AGPLv3 requires sharing source code of modifications used in networked services
Smaller community than some general-purpose streaming servers

faqs.question: Is MistServer free?
faqs.answer: The open-source edition is free under AGPLv3; commercial editions and licenses are paid.

faqs.question: Who makes MistServer?
faqs.answer: DDVTech B.V., a Dutch streaming technology company founded in 2009.

faqs.question: What protocols does MistServer support?
faqs.answer: WebRTC, WHIP, SRT, RIST, HLS, and others.

faqs.question: Can I use MistServer commercially without open-sourcing my code?
faqs.answer: Yes, by purchasing a commercial license.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted OTT streaming infrastructure
use_cases.description: Run your own live-streaming delivery stack across multiple protocols.

use_cases.title: Enterprise streaming platform
use_cases.description: Deploy MistServer with a commercial license for vendor-backed support.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: MistServer is a streaming media toolkit with a free open-source edition (AGPLv3) and commercial licensing for OTT video delivery.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source audio/video casting system that turns a Raspberry Pi or Linux device into a Chromecast-style receiver.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: NymphCast is an open-source audio and video casting system that turns a Raspberry Pi or other Linux-capable device into a receiver similar to Chromecast, controlled from a phone or PC client. It also supports custom NymphCast Apps that extend receiver functionality.
The project remains under active development but has stayed in alpha status for an extended period; the stable release is v0.1, with v0.2 and features like SmartTV support still being built out.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: NymphCast is aimed at hobbyists and privacy-conscious self-hosters who want an open, DIY casting receiver built on inexpensive hardware instead of a proprietary streaming device.

features.title: Audio/Video Casting
features.description: Cast audio and video from phone or PC clients to a NymphCast receiver.

features.title: Runs on Raspberry Pi & Linux
features.description: Turns low-cost, Linux-capable hardware into a streaming receiver.

features.title: Custom App Support
features.description: NymphCast Apps extend what the receiver can do beyond basic casting.

features.title: Fully Open Hardware & Software
features.description: Both the client and receiver software are open source.

features.title: Cross-Client Control
features.description: NymphCast Player apps let you browse and cast from phone or PC.

pros: Fully open source with no vendor lock-in
Runs on affordable hardware like Raspberry Pi
Actively developed with regular releases
Open alternative to proprietary casting ecosystems
cons: Project has remained in alpha status for an extended period
Some features, such as SmartTV support and NymphCast Apps, are only partially complete
Requires DIY hardware setup and technical comfort

faqs.question: Is NymphCast ready for everyday use?
faqs.answer: It's still in alpha; the stable release is v0.1, with v0.2 in active development.

faqs.question: What hardware does NymphCast run on?
faqs.answer: Raspberry Pi and other Linux-capable devices.

faqs.question: Is NymphCast free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is fully open source.

faqs.question: How do I cast to a NymphCast receiver?
faqs.answer: Using NymphCast client/player apps for phone or PC.

use_cases.title: DIY casting receiver
use_cases.description: Build a Chromecast-style receiver on a Raspberry Pi.

use_cases.title: Open-source home casting
use_cases.description: Cast media at home without a proprietary streaming device.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: NymphCast is an open-source audio/video casting system that turns a Raspberry Pi or Linux device into a Chromecast-style receiver.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source UPnP/DLNA media server and renderer built for the GNOME desktop.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Rygel is a home media solution that implements both a UPnP AV MediaServer and MediaRenderer, letting you share audio, video, and pictures across a home network and control playback on other devices. It is part of the GNOME project, written largely in Vala, and released under the LGPL-2.0 license.

Through its plug-in architecture, Rygel can stream media from a PC to TVs, game consoles, and DLNA speakers, with optional on-the-fly transcoding for device compatibility. It also provides developer libraries (librygel-core, librygel-server, librygel-renderer) for building custom UPnP/DLNA applications.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Rygel is built for Linux and GNOME desktop users who want to share a local media library over UPnP/DLNA, and for developers building custom UPnP/DLNA server or renderer applications.

features.title: UPnP MediaServer & MediaRenderer
features.description: Combines both server and renderer roles in one plugin-based application.

features.title: Home Network Media Sharing
features.description: Shares audio, video, and pictures with other devices on the network.

features.title: On-the-Fly Transcoding
features.description: Converts media formats for compatibility with the requesting device.

features.title: DLNA Speaker Redirection
features.description: Redirects audio playback to DLNA-compatible speakers.

features.title: Developer Libraries
features.description: librygel-core, librygel-server, and librygel-renderer for building custom UPnP apps.

features.title: GStreamer Integration
features.description: Uses GStreamer for media handling and playback.

pros: Free and open source (LGPL-2.0)
Tightly integrated with the GNOME desktop
Works as both a media server and a media renderer
Available in most Linux distribution repositories
cons: Primarily targets Linux/GNOME rather than Windows or macOS
UPnP/DLNA setup can require additional network configuration
Documentation is developer-oriented rather than beginner-friendly

faqs.question: Is Rygel free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is free and open source under the LGPL-2.0 license.

faqs.question: What is Rygel used for?
faqs.answer: Sharing and streaming media over a home network via UPnP/DLNA.

faqs.question: Does Rygel work outside of GNOME?
faqs.answer: It's built for GNOME but can run on other Linux desktops with the required libraries.

faqs.question: Can Rygel act as a media renderer, not just a server?
faqs.answer: Yes, it supports both MediaServer and MediaRenderer roles.

use_cases.title: Home media sharing
use_cases.description: Share a Linux PC's media library with smart TVs and DLNA devices.

use_cases.title: Custom UPnP/DLNA app development
use_cases.description: Build custom media server or renderer applications using Rygel's libraries.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Rygel is a free, open-source UPnP/DLNA media server and renderer built for the GNOME desktop and Linux home networks.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source self-hosted app for organizing and browsing a personal adult video and image library.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Stash is a free, self-hosted application for organizing and viewing a personal collection of adult video and image files. It is developed by volunteers and released under the AGPL license, with the project describing it as running entirely on your own network with no content sent to external services.

Stash supports rating, tagging, and marking scenes and images, building galleries from folders or zip files, and pulling metadata either from filenames or community-curated scrapers. It streams to any modern browser on PC, tablet, or phone and can be password-protected.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Stash is aimed at individuals who already have a personal media collection and want a private, self-hosted way to organize, tag, and browse it without relying on cloud services.

features.title: Private Media Library Organizer
features.description: Organizes a personal video and image collection on your own network.

features.title: Tagging, Rating & Markers
features.description: Rate and tag scenes and images by performer, studio, movie, or custom tag; bookmark specific moments with markers.

features.title: Metadata Scraping
features.description: Pulls metadata from filenames or community-curated scrapers.

features.title: Gallery Support
features.description: Builds galleries from folders or zip files of images.

features.title: Cross-Device Streaming
features.description: Stream your library to a browser on PC, tablet, or phone.

features.title: Password Protection
features.description: Optional password protection for added privacy.

pros: Free and open source under the AGPL license
Runs entirely on your own network with no data sent externally
Flexible tagging and metadata system
Active volunteer development community
cons: You must supply your own media — no content is included
Scraper and metadata configuration can be technical to set up
Narrow focus limits it to a specific content type

faqs.question: Is Stash free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is free and open source under the AGPL license.

faqs.question: Does Stash host or provide any content?
faqs.answer: No, it only organizes media you already own.

faqs.question: Can I password-protect my Stash instance?
faqs.answer: Yes.

faqs.question: What platforms can I view Stash on?
faqs.answer: Any modern web browser on PC, tablet, or phone.

use_cases.title: Personal media library organization
use_cases.description: Tag, rate, and browse an existing personal media collection.
use_cases.audience: Individuals with existing local media libraries

use_cases.title: Private self-hosted alternative to cloud organizers
use_cases.description: Keep a personal collection off cloud services entirely.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Stash is a free, open-source self-hosted application for organizing and browsing a personal adult video and image library.
